summary: DeVault argues that regular expressions are overused for validation tasks where they're almost always the wrong tool. Using URL validation as a case study, he shows how even a heavily-revised Stack Overflow regex fails to match many valid URLs while accepting invalid ones. He demonstrates that purpose-built parsers and standard library functions are superior to regex for structured data validation. The post concludes with a practical list of common validation tasks and their proper non-regex solutions.
key_points: A top Stack Overflow URL regex, revised 9 times, still fails on valid URLs like short domains, internationalized domains, IPv6, and URLs with special characters
Complicated regexes are opaque, unmaintainable, and often wrong — making debugging nearly impossible when bugs are reported
Standard library parsers (like Python's urlparse) are the correct approach for validating structured formats
Regexes are only appropriate for validating simple patterns or finding patterns in text, not for complex validation logic
For each common validation task (email, passwords, dates, credit cards), a domain-specific solution exists that outperforms regex
money_quotes: Complicated regexes are opaque, unmaintainable, and often wrong.
This answer has been revised 9 times on Stack Overflow, and this is the best they could come up with.
If you received a bug report in your application because one of these URLs was handled incorrectly, do you understand this regex well enough to fix it?
A regex is useful for validating simple patterns and for finding patterns in text. For anything beyond that it's almost certainly a terrible choice.
validate an email address: try to send an email to it!
key_insight: Regexes are overused for validation tasks where purpose-built parsers and standard library functions would be simpler, more correct, and more maintainable.
